items.title: Spam filter with word counts
items.scenario: An email contains "free" twice, "win" once, and "meeting" once. A Multinomial NB spam model trained on thousands of labeled emails assigns high θ_spam,free and θ_spam,win. The log-score for spam includes 2·log θ_spam,free + log θ_spam,win, outweighing the ham class.
items.explanation: Repeated spammy tokens increase the Multinomial score linearly in their counts—exactly what count-based features encode.

items.title: Short product review sentiment
items.scenario: Reviews are one sentence: "Not bad" vs "Not good." A Bernoulli NB model tracks whether words like "good" and "bad" appear, not how many times. The absence of "good" in the second review contributes via (1 − θ_pos,good).
items.explanation: Bernoulli NB uses both presence and absence probabilities—useful when documents are short and repetition is rare.

items.title: News topic tagging at scale
items.scenario: A media company routes wire stories into sports, politics, or finance using a vocabulary of 20k stemmed tokens. Multinomial NB trains in seconds on CPU, serves predictions in milliseconds, and gives per-word log-odds for debugging mislabels.
items.explanation: Before transformer fine-tuning, Multinomial NB was a production workhorse for high-volume text routing because training and inference are cheap and transparent.
